The relevant articles in the Single Supervisory Mechanism Regulation (SSMR) that deal with the establishment of an Administrative Board of Review and its operating rules can be found in Articles 25 to 30. These articles outline the composition, tasks, powers, and procedures of the Administrative Board of Review, which serves as an independent body responsible for reviewing decisions made by the European Central Bank (ECB) in the context of the Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM). The operating rules of the Administrative Board of Review are further detailed in Regulation (EU) No 1093/2013.

In the context of the European Union's Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM), the separation principle is implemented to maintain independence, objectivity, and impartiality in decision-making processes. This is achieved by structuring the ECB's organization so that its supervisory tasks are functionally separated from other activities within the financial institution.

This separation helps minimize potential conflicts of interest, maintains public trust in the supervision process, and promotes effective risk management. The SSM Regulation requires the ECB to establish such a clear organizational structure to ensure that it can carry out its supervisory functions effectively and in accordance with EU law. This is done by sharing different elements of banking supervision across multiple directorates general and one directorate within the ECB, each reporting functionally to the Chair and Vice-Chair of the Supervisory Board.

message: Yes, you are correct. The Directorate General Horizontal Line Supervision (DG/HOL) plays a crucial role in the European Central Bank's Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM). It ensures consistent supervision across all banks under its jurisdiction by overseeing the application of horizontal supervisory requirements, such as prudential rules, risk management practices, and governance standards. DG/HOL also contributes to the development of supervisory policies, strategies, and methodologies within the ECB, works closely with other directorates general and the Supervisory Board, engages in dialogue with various stakeholders, and ensures compliance with relevant EU laws. In summary, DG/HOL maintains consistency and coordination of the ECB's supervision across all banks under its jurisdiction, contributes to the development of supervisory policies and strategies within the SSM, and ensures compliance with EU law.

message: The objectives for the management body of an institution can vary depending on the nature and type of the institution, but some common objectives include strategic planning, resource allocation, risk management, compliance, stakeholder engagement, performance monitoring, governance, innovation, reputation management, succession planning, and ensuring that the management body is suitable (fit and proper) to carry out its responsibilities. This includes contributing to effective management of the institution and balanced decision-making, which impacts not only the safety and soundness of the institution itself but also the wider banking sector. The level of supervisory engagement varies depending on the overall risk profile of the institution.

In the provided context, the two main categories of costs incurred for supervision are not explicitly referred to as operational costs and capital costs. However, they can be inferred as follows:

1. Direct supervision and indirect supervision costs can be considered operational costs. These costs include expenses related to staffing, IT systems, data collection, on-site inspections, and day-to-day supervisory activities (as mentioned in sections 2.3, 2.4, and 3 Supervision of all supervised entities).

2. The expenditure incurred by the ECB for the conduct of supervisory tasks consists primarily of the direct expenses of ECB Banking Supervision and the shared services utilized for the supervisory function. This expenditure can be considered capital costs, as it includes investment in infrastructure such as developing methodologies, policies, and guidelines for effective supervision (as mentioned in Supervisory Manual – Supervision of all supervised entities).

These costs are essential for ensuring that the institution operates efficiently and effectively while adhering to regulatory requirements.

However, it is expected that institutions will ensure their management bodies have the necessary skills, knowledge, and expertise to effectively manage the institution and make sound decisions. This can include theoretical banking experience, as well as practical experience in areas such as risk management, finance, governance, and compliance. The specific requirements may vary depending on the nature and complexity of the institution, as well as any applicable national or international regulations.

message: Yes, you are correct. The European Court of Auditors (ECA) primarily focuses on auditing the EU's financial management. This includes auditing the financial statements of the EU institutions, examining the implementation of EU financial assistance programs, assessing the effectiveness of internal controls, and evaluating the performance of EU agencies and bodies. The ECA also provides independent opinions on the annual reports of the European Central Bank (ECB) and the European Systemic Risk Board (ESRB). However, it's important to note that in respect of the ECB, the ECA's audit powers are limited to the examination of the operational efficiency of the management of the Supervisory Manual – Functioning of the Single Supervisory Mechanism due to the ECB's independence.

Yes, your understanding of the role of the European Commission in preparing financial services legislation within the European Union (EU) is correct. The Commission proposes new laws, regulations, and policies related to financial services, ensuring they are aligned with the EU's objectives and principles, such as maintaining financial stability, promoting fairness, protecting consumers, and fostering competition in the financial sector.

The Commission also coordinates the implementation of these legislative acts across EU Member States and monitors their effectiveness. Furthermore, it collaborates with other EU institutions, such as the European Parliament and the Council, to ensure that proposed legislation is approved and implemented effectively.

In addition, the Commission interacts with the European Central Bank (ECB) mainly for gathering supervision and adopts delegated and implementing acts, including technical standards prepared by the European Supervisory Authorities, to complement EU financial services legislation. The ECB may also provide input to the Commission on regulatory issues that are relevant and necessary for the performance of its prudential tasks.

Lastly, the SSM (Single Supervisory Mechanism) cooperates closely with other European institutions and bodies as part of a wider institutional framework. This includes working with the Directorate-General for Financial Stability, Financial Services and Capital Markets Union (DG FISMA) within the European Commission.

message: In the context of a European Central Bank (ECB) supervisory procedure, parties involved have the right to request access to the supervisory file under certain conditions. This right is outlined in Article 10 of Regulation (EU) No 1024/2013 on information requirements and cooperation between competent authorities. The right to access the supervisory file allows parties to challenge ECB's decisions and ensure that their rights are protected. However, this right is subject to certain limitations such as protecting confidential information, maintaining financial stability, and preserving the integrity of the supervisory process. The specific conditions for accessing the supervisory file may vary depending on the nature of the case and the applicable regulations. This includes the ECB Regulation on reporting of supervisory financial information (ECB/2015/13), ECB Decision on the provision to the ECB of supervisory data reported to the national competent authorities by the supervised entities (ECB/2023/18), and ECB Decision on the reporting of funding plans of supervised entities by national competent authorities to the ECB (ECB/2023/19).

message:
In the context of the European Union, various tools are used for cooperation between institutions. Some common tools include:

1. Memorandums of Understanding (MoUs): These are agreements between institutions that outline their roles, responsibilities, and areas of cooperation in a particular policy area or project. For example, MoUs may be established between national supervisory authorities and the European Central Bank (ECB) to facilitate information sharing and coordination in banking supervision.

2. Joint Committees: These are established to ensure effective collaboration on specific policy areas or projects. For example, the Joint Committee under the Banking Union agreement oversees the functioning of the Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM) and the Single Resolution Mechanism (SRM).

3. Coordination Groups: These are established to ensure that different institutions work together effectively on specific issues or projects. For example, the Eurogroup is a coordination group of euro area finance ministers who meet regularly to discuss economic and financial policy matters affecting the euro area.

4. Working Groups: These are temporary groups set up to address specific issues or projects. They may include representatives from various institutions and work together to produce recommendations or reports on their assigned topic.

5. Exchanges of Information: Institutions share information with each other to ensure coordination, transparency, and consistency in policy-making and decision-making processes. This can be done through formal channels such as the European System of Central Banks (ESCB) or informal networks like the Governors' Club.

6. Joint Actions: In some cases, institutions may take joint actions to address common challenges or achieve shared objectives. For example, the ECB and national supervisory authorities may coordinate their supervision of a specific bank operating across multiple EU countries.

7. Delegated and Implementing Acts: The European Commission may delegate certain powers to European Supervisory Authorities (ESAs) to develop technical standards that complement EU financial services legislation. This ensures consistency in the application of EU law across Member States.

The text you provided also mentions institution-specific tools like MoUs and written coordination and cooperation agreements (WCCAs), which are used for cooperation within supervisory colleges, as well as a framework established by the ECB in 2022 to facilitate information exchange with other authorities. However, these tools do not fit neatly into the categories listed above since they are specific to banking supervision and cooperation among supervisors across borders and sectors.

message:
In the context of the Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM), the Joint Supervisory Teams (JSTs) are primarily responsible for monitoring and assessing the remuneration policies and practices of a supervised entity to ensure they are consistent with the SSM Guidelines on remuneration, promote sound risk management, and prevent excessive risk-taking. They also verify that these policies and practices comply with relevant EU legislation such as the Capital Requirements Directive (CRD) and the Capital Requirements Regulation (CRR).

The specific responsibilities of each JST may vary but generally include:
1. Assessing the remuneration policy and practices to ensure they are consistent with the SSM Guidelines on remuneration.
2. Identifying potential risks associated with these policies and practices, such as excessive risk-taking or inadequate risk management.
3. Ensuring that the remuneration policies and practices align with the overall strategy and business model of the supervised entity.
4. Monitoring changes in the remuneration policy and practices to ensure they remain consistent with the SSM Guidelines on remuneration and relevant EU legislation.
5. Taking appropriate supervisory action if the remuneration policies and practices are found to be non-compliant with the SSM Guidelines on remuneration or relevant EU legislation.
6. Collaborating with other JSTs, national competent authorities, and the European Central Bank (ECB) to ensure consistent application of the SSM Guidelines on remuneration across all supervised entities.

message:
In the context of the Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM), the European Central Bank (ECB) plays a key role in supervisory colleges for significant banking groups. The ECB is responsible for coordinating and overseeing the work of the Joint Supervisory Teams (JSTs) within these colleges.

The specific roles of the ECB include:
1. Setting the overall strategy and direction for the supervision of significant banking groups.
2. Ensuring that the JSTs follow the SSM Guidelines on remuneration, risk assessment, and other relevant areas.
3. Facilitating information exchange between the JSTs to ensure consistent application of supervisory standards across all members of a significant banking group.
4. Providing guidance and support to the JSTs in their supervision of significant banking groups.
5. Taking appropriate supervisory action if necessary, such as issuing warnings or imposing sanctions on a supervised entity that is found to be non-compliant with relevant EU legislation or SSM Guidelines.
6. Collaborating with other European Supervisory Authorities (ESAs) and national competent authorities to ensure consistent application of supervisory standards across the EU.
7. Reporting on the supervision of significant banking groups to the Governing Council, the Supervisory Board, and the European Parliament as required by applicable regulations.

message:
In the context of the Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM), significant institutions are required to follow a specific process for notifying the European Central Bank (ECB) and their home supervisor about certain decisions that may impact their financial stability or compliance with EU legislation. This process is outlined in Article 4(5) of Regulation (EU) No 1024/2013 on information requirements and cooperation between competent authorities.

The significant institution must provide a written notification that includes:
1. A clear description of the decision to be made, including its purpose, scope, and potential impact on the financial stability or compliance of the institution.
2. The reasons for making the decision, taking into account relevant EU legislation, SSM Guidelines, and best practices in banking supervision.
3. An assessment of the risks associated with the decision, including potential impacts on financial stability, compliance, and reputation.
4. Any alternative options that have been considered and the reasons why they were not chosen.
5. The expected timeline for implementing the decision, as well as any contingency plans in case unforeseen circumstances arise.
6. Any other relevant information that may help the ECB and home supervisor to assess the decision and its potential impact on financial stability or compliance.

The significant institution must provide this notification at least 10 business days before the decision is made, unless otherwise agreed upon with the ECB and home supervisor. The ECB and home supervisor may then request additional information or take appropriate supervisory action if necessary to ensure that the decision complies with relevant EU legislation and SSM Guidelines.

This process helps to promote transparency, consistency, and coordination in banking supervision across the EU, ensuring financial stability and maintaining trust in the European banking system.

message:
In the context of the Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM), a credit institution's authorization can cease to exist under several circumstances, primarily due to non-compliance with relevant EU legislation or SSM Guidelines. Some examples include:

1. Insolvency: If a credit institution becomes insolvent and is unable to meet its financial obligations, the national competent authority may revoke its authorization.

2. Breach of Regulatory Requirements: If a credit institution repeatedly breaches regulatory requirements or fails to address significant deficiencies identified by the European Central Bank (ECB) or home supervisor, the ECB may decide to withdraw the institution's authorization.

3. Financial Stability Concerns: If a credit institution poses a threat to financial stability due to its size, interconnectedness, or risk profile, the ECB may decide to revoke its authorization to protect the broader European banking system.

4. Mergers and Acquisitions: In some cases, a credit institution's authorization may cease to exist as a result of mergers or acquisitions. The ECB and home supervisor must approve such transactions to ensure that they comply with relevant EU legislation and promote financial stability.

5. Withdrawal of Authorization: The ECB has the power to withdraw a credit institution's authorization if it determines that the institution no longer meets the conditions for obtaining or maintaining its authorization, even if the institution has not become insolvent or breached regulatory requirements. This can occur in cases where the institution is no longer viable or poses a threat to financial stability.

6. Other Circumstances: There may be other circumstances under which a credit institution's authorization can cease to exist, such as if the institution engages in illegal activities, fails to maintain adequate capital levels, or violates anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing regulations.

The revocation of a credit institution's authorization is a significant action that can have far-reaching consequences for the institution, its customers, and the broader European banking system. The ECB and home supervisor must carefully consider all relevant factors before making such a decision to ensure financial stability and maintain trust in the European banking system.

In the context of the Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM), procedural acts during the European Central Bank's (ECB) banking supervision process are typically notified by the Joint Supervisory Teams (JSTs). The procedure for these notifications involves the following steps:

1. The JST responsible for the supervision of the relevant supervised entity identifies a procedural act that requires notification to the ECB.
2. The JST prepares a written notification that includes all necessary information about the procedural act, such as its purpose, scope, potential impact on financial stability or compliance, and any alternative options considered.
3. The JST sends the notification to the ECB's Supervisory Coordination Unit (SCU) at least 10 business days before the procedural act is carried out, unless otherwise agreed upon with the SCU.
4. The SCU reviews the notification and may request additional information or clarification if necessary.
5. If the SCU determines that the procedural act complies with relevant EU legislation and SSM Guidelines, it will not take any further action. However, if the SCU identifies concerns or issues, it may provide guidance or recommendations to the JST responsible for supervision.
6. The JST must consider the feedback from the SCU before proceeding with the procedural act. If necessary, the JST may revise its approach or take additional measures to address any identified concerns or issues.
7. Once the procedural act has been carried out, the JST responsible for supervision provides a follow-up notification to the SCU, summarizing the outcome of the act and any actions taken as a result.

This procedure helps to promote transparency, consistency, and coordination in banking supervision across the EU, ensuring financial stability and maintaining trust in the European banking system.

Yes, in the context of banking supervision procedures, a good reputation for an appointee is typically determined based on several factors as you've listed:

1. Professional qualifications and experience: The appointee's educational background, professional certifications, and relevant work experience in the financial sector are important considerations.
2. Integrity and honesty: The appointee's personal character, including their integrity, honesty, and ethical behavior, is assessed to ensure they meet high moral standards.
3. Compliance with laws and regulations: The appointee's compliance with relevant laws, regulations, and industry best practices is evaluated to ensure they have a strong understanding of the regulatory environment and can adhere to it.
4. Financial soundness: The appointee's financial stability and solvency are assessed to ensure they do not pose any risks to the institution or the broader financial system.
5. Reputation in the industry: The appointee's reputation within the financial sector is evaluated based on feedback from peers, customers, and other stakeholders. Any negative incidents or controversies associated with the appointee may be taken into account.
6. Conflicts of interest: Potential conflicts of interest are assessed to ensure that the appointee can perform their duties objectively and without undue influence.
7. Other relevant factors: Additional factors, such as the appointee's ability to work collaboratively, adaptability, and commitment to the institution's mission and values, may also be considered when determining whether they have a good reputation.

These factors help banking supervisors to ensure that appointees meet high standards of integrity, competence, and financial soundness, which are essential for maintaining trust in the European banking system.

In the context of the Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM), liquidity measures can be enforced under specific conditions that consider both quantitative and qualitative aspects. These conditions are primarily aimed at ensuring financial stability and maintaining trust in the European banking system.

1. Quantitative Aspects: Liquidity measures may be enforced when a credit institution fails to meet minimum liquidity requirements, such as the Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) or the Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR). These ratios are designed to ensure that institutions have sufficient high-quality liquid assets and stable funding sources to withstand a 30-day stress scenario.

2. Qualitative Aspects: Liquidity measures may also be enforced when a credit institution demonstrates weaknesses in its internal liquidity risk management framework, such as inadequate stress testing, poor liquidity risk identification and monitoring, or deficiencies in the management of liquidity risks associated with specific activities or products.

In both cases, the European Central Bank (ECB) and home supervisor will assess the situation and determine whether enforcement action is necessary to protect financial stability and maintain trust in the European banking system. The ECB Regulation on reporting of supervisory financial information (ECB/2015/13), the ECB Decision on the provision to the ECB of supervisory data reported to the national competent authorities by the supervised entities (ECB/2023/18), and the ECB Decision on the reporting of funding plans of supervised entities by national competent authorities to the ECB (ECB/2023/19) might be used in the assessment, implementation, and monitoring phases respectively.

message:
The question asks for a summary of the steps involved in the non-objection procedure when a decision has significant implications for supervised entities at the European Central Bank (ECB). Here's a breakdown of the process:

1. Draft Decision Preparation: The ECB's Executive Board prepares a draft decision that has significant implications for supervised entities, taking into account relevant factors such as financial stability, trust in the European banking system, and emerging trends and challenges in the banking sector.

2. Consultation with the Supervisory Board: The draft decision is presented to the Supervisory Board for consultation, allowing members to provide feedback and suggestions before it is finalized.

3. Governing Council Meeting: The draft decision is then discussed during a meeting of the Governing Council, where members have the opportunity to express their views and ask questions about the proposal.

4. Non-Objection Procedure: If no objections are raised during the discussion, the decision can be adopted without further action. However, if an objection is raised, the Executive Board may revise the draft decision or engage in additional discussions with the objecting member to address their concerns.

5. Final Decision Adoption: Once a consensus has been reached and no further objections are raised, the final decision can be adopted by the Governing Council. The decision is then communicated to the relevant supervised entities and implemented accordingly.

6. Monitoring and Enforcement: The ECB and home supervisor will monitor the implementation of the decision and take enforcement actions if necessary to ensure compliance with the new requirements or standards.

The non-objection procedure helps promote transparency, consistency, and coordination in banking supervision across the EU, ensuring financial stability and maintaining trust in the European banking system.

message:
The European Central Bank (ECB) evaluates the potential impact of a failure of a Large Systemically Important Institution (LSI) on both the domestic and regional financial system by considering several key factors. These include:

1. Size: The bank's total assets, balance sheet, and market capitalization are assessed to determine its scale relative to the overall financial sector.
2. Importance to the local economy: The institution's role in providing credit, facilitating payments, and serving as a financial intermediary is evaluated to understand its contribution to the local economy.
3. Cross-border activities: The bank's involvement in international transactions, subsidiaries, branches, or other cross-border operations is analyzed to determine its impact on multiple jurisdictions.
4. Business model: The institution's primary business lines, such as retail banking, investment banking, and insurance, are examined to understand the potential contagion risks associated with each activity.
5. Interconnectedness: The bank's relationships with other financial institutions, both domestically and internationally, are assessed to determine its role in the broader financial network.
6. Financial instruments: The types of assets and liabilities held by the institution, as well as their maturity, liquidity, and marketability, are evaluated to understand the potential for losses or disruptions in case of failure.
7. Risk management practices: The bank's risk management framework, including its stress testing, scenario analysis, and internal controls, is assessed to determine its ability to manage risks effectively.

By considering these factors, the ECB can develop a comprehensive understanding of the potential systemic risks posed by an LSI and take appropriate measures to mitigate those risks, ensuring financial stability and maintaining trust in the European banking system.

Institutions categorized as higher-risk levels are expected to maintain stronger capital buffers, more liquid assets, and robust internal controls to mitigate potential risks to financial stability.

Yes, you are correct. The European Central Bank (ECB) engages in several ongoing projects aimed at improving the internal modeling of various risk categories. One such project is the development and enhancement of the Supervisory Review and Evaluation Process (SREP), which provides a framework for assessing institutions based on their risk profile.

The ECB also invests in research and innovation to advance its understanding of emerging risks, such as cybersecurity threats or climate change-related risks, and develop appropriate modeling techniques to incorporate these factors into the SREP.

Another project is the ongoing work on the development of a common EU approach for the calculation of countercyclical capital buffers (CCyB). This initiative aims to ensure that banks hold sufficient capital during periods of credit growth to absorb potential losses in case of an economic downturn, thereby contributing to financial stability.

The ECB collaborates with other European supervisory authorities on projects such as the Joint Supervisory Teams (JSTs), which facilitate coordinated supervision of cross-border banking groups and contribute to a consistent assessment of risk across the EU. These ongoing projects help the ECB to continuously improve its internal modeling of various risk categories, ensuring that it can effectively assess institutions' risk profiles and take appropriate measures to maintain financial stability within the European Union.

message:
During college meetings (Supervisory Review and Evaluation Process or SREP), the European Central Bank (ECB) evaluates various aspects of a banking group or entity to determine its risk profile. This evaluation can be both quantitative and qualitative in nature.

Quantitative assessments may include analyzing financial indicators such as capital adequacy ratios, liquidity ratios, and leverage ratios. The ECB also examines the institution's asset quality, including loan loss provisions, non-performing loans, and credit risk exposures. Additionally, the bank's profitability, solvency, and efficiency are assessed using key performance indicators (KPIs) such as return on equity (ROE), cost-to-income ratios, and net interest margins.

Qualitative assessments focus on the institution's risk management practices, governance structure, internal controls, and compliance with regulatory requirements. The ECB evaluates the effectiveness of the bank's risk identification, measurement, and mitigation processes, as well as its ability to manage operational risks, market risks, and credit risks effectively. The quality of the institution's risk data, stress testing capabilities, and scenario analysis are also assessed during this process.

The ECB's evaluation process is designed to provide a comprehensive understanding of an institution's risk profile, enabling it to take appropriate supervisory actions to maintain financial stability within the European Union.

Yes, you are correct. A divergent policy choice can be made within the context of EU law when there is a justification for deviating from the general principles while still ensuring compliance with the overall objectives of the legislation. The ECB, together with national competent authorities (NCAs), has developed policies regarding fit and proper criteria, and supervisory practices and processes that explain how European banking supervision applies on a case-by-case basis.

These policies are adopted without prejudice to national law and in compliance with the EBA Guidelines. The justification for deviating from the general principles of equal treatment, proportionality, and legitimate expectations should be clearly articulated and supported by evidence, ensuring that the decision is transparent, accountable, and consistent with the broader objectives of EU law.

In cases where a divergent policy choice is made, the ECB must ensure that the decision does not create undue burdens or disadvantages for any supervised entity and that it contributes to maintaining financial stability within the European Union. The ECB may also need to engage in dialogue with national competent authorities and other relevant stakeholders to address concerns and ensure a coordinated approach to supervision across the EU.

In addition, the separation principle encompasses rules on information-sharing between the ECB’s two functions (monetary policy and supervisory tasks). Confidential information can be shared upon request and on a need-to-know basis, but one function should not disclose confidential information containing assessments or policy recommendations to the other without approval.

In summary, a divergent policy choice can be made when justified by specific circumstances, as long as it is proportionate, transparent, accountable, and contributes to maintaining financial stability within the EU. The ECB must ensure compliance with the general principles of EU law, engage in dialogue with relevant stakeholders, and adhere to rules on information-sharing between its functions.

Yes, your understanding of thematic reviews in banking supervision is correct. They are conducted by the European Central Bank (ECB) to investigate potential breaches of prudential rules and regulations by significant institutions. The focus is on specific areas or topics that pose a risk to financial stability, such as credit risk management, operational risk management, or liquidity risk management.

The purpose of thematic reviews is to identify common weaknesses across multiple institutions, assess the effectiveness of supervisory practices and processes, and develop targeted remedial actions to address systemic risks within the banking sector. This helps ensure that significant institutions comply with prudential rules and regulations, maintain strong risk management frameworks, and contribute to maintaining financial stability within the European Union.

In addition, thematic reviews allow the institution to understand how it has been assessed and which areas are in need of improvement. The ECB may also organize additional meetings with the industry, such as CEO workshops on dedicated topics, to gather further insights.

The technical system that enables Joint Supervisory Teams (JSTs) to consistently apply a common methodology and standards in banking supervision during their horizontal in-depth risk analysis activities is primarily based on the following elements:

1. Shared methodologies, guidelines, and best practices: The ECB develops and provides JSTs with shared methodologies, guidelines, and best practices to ensure consistency across all teams when conducting risk assessments. These documents outline the steps, criteria, and expectations for each phase of the assessment process.

2. Collaborative platform: A collaborative platform is used by JST members to share information, exchange ideas, and coordinate their efforts during the assessment process. This platform facilitates communication between team members from different national competent authorities (NCAs) and allows them to work together effectively.

3. Regular meetings and workshops: JSTs hold regular meetings and workshops to discuss ongoing assessments, share findings, and address any challenges or issues that may arise during the process. These meetings help ensure consistency in the application of methodologies and standards across all teams.

4. Training and development programs: The ECB provides training and development programs for JST members to enhance their skills and knowledge in various areas relevant to banking supervision, such as risk assessment methodologies, regulatory requirements, and emerging risks. This ensures that team members have the necessary expertise to conduct consistent assessments.

5. Peer review process: The ECB conducts peer reviews of JSTs to evaluate the quality and consistency of their work, identify areas for improvement, and provide feedback on best practices. These reviews help ensure that all teams are adhering to common methodologies and standards in their risk assessments.

6. Ongoing monitoring and evaluation: The ECB continuously monitors and evaluates the performance of JSTs to ensure that they are meeting their objectives effectively. This includes tracking the progress of ongoing assessments, analyzing the results of completed reviews, and making adjustments as needed to improve the overall process.

In summary, the technical system that enables JSTs to consistently apply a common methodology and standards in banking supervision is based on shared methodologies, guidelines, best practices, collaborative platforms, regular meetings and workshops, training and development programs, peer review processes, and ongoing monitoring and evaluation. This ensures consistency across all teams when conducting risk assessments, contributing to the maintenance of financial stability within the European Union.

Periodic penalty payments can be enforced by the European Central Bank (ECB) as a coercive measure to compel compliance with obligations that have been imposed due to a breach of an ECB decision or regulation. This is done with the goal of ensuring adherence to regulatory obligations and maintaining financial stability within the European Union. The use of such measures is based on the principle of proportionality, ensuring that the measure is appropriate and necessary given the nature and severity of the breach, as well as the potential impact on financial stability. The ECB will carefully consider the specific circumstances of each case before deciding to impose a penalty payment as a coercive measure.

Periodic penalty payments 
The enforcement measures directly available to the ECB are known as “periodic 
penalty payments”. Periodic penalty payments are not intended to punish the 
supervised person concerned. They are enforced when a breach of an ECB decision 
or regulation is still ongoing in order to compel the supervised person concerned to

comply with the obligations that have been imposed. 
Periodic penalty payments may be imposed on significant institutions and on less 
significant institutions if the relevant ECB decision or regulation imposes obligations 
on less significant institutions vis-à-vis the ECB. They need to be effective and 
proportionate. The total amount that a supervised entity may be obliged to pay in the 
form of a periodic penalty payment is based on the number of days that elapse until

it fully complies with the ECB decision or regulation breached. Periodic penalty 
payments may be imposed for a maximum period of six months from the date 
stipulated in the relevant decision or regulation. The upper limit on periodic penalty 
payments shall be 5% of the average daily turnover of the supervised entity 
concerned per day of infringement.

Periodic penalty payments imposed by the ECB are published on the ECB’s banking

method of setting administrative pecuniary penalties. The penalties imposed must be 
effective, proportionate and dissuasive.

Activities subject to the European Central Bank (ECB)'s supervision primarily include those related to credit institutions such as banks, investment firms, and insurance companies. These entities play a crucial role in the financial system and their stability is essential for maintaining financial stability within the European Union.

State aid conditionality comes into play when these institutions receive financial assistance from public authorities, either directly or indirectly. In such cases, the ECB may impose conditions on the aid to ensure that it does not distort competition or create undue burdens on other market participants. These conditions are designed to promote the effective use of the aid and its alignment with the overall objectives of the Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM).

The ECB's supervision covers various aspects of these institutions, including their governance structures, risk management practices, capital adequacy, liquidity management, and solvency. The ECB ensures that these entities comply with relevant EU legislation such as the Capital Requirements Directive (CRD) and the Capital Requirements Regulation (CRR), as well as other prudential rules and regulations.

The documents you provided, like the ECB Regulation on reporting of supervisory financial information (ECB/2015/13), ECB Decision on the provision to the ECB of supervisory data reported to the national competent authorities by the supervised entities (ECB/2023/18), and ECB Decision on the reporting of funding plans of supervised entities by national competent authorities to the ECB (ECB/2023/19) are examples of measures taken by the ECB for its supervisory role.

The Supervisory Manual – Supervision of significant institutions is a guide that outlines the ECB's powers and procedures for supervising these entities, including the power to require significant institutions to take necessary measures if a breach of prudential requirements has occurred (4.10.1 Supervisory measures). The ECB also has the power to waive compliance with certain prudential requirements laid down in the CRR for an institution and all or some of its branches (4.8.2 Capital and liquidity waivers).

In summary, the ECB's supervision is crucial for maintaining financial stability within the European Union, and it covers various aspects of credit institutions, investment firms, and insurance companies. State aid conditionality may be required when these entities receive financial assistance from public authorities to ensure that the aid is used effectively and does not distort competition or create undue burdens on other market participants.

The risk assessment system supports Joint Supervisory Teams (JSTs) in their analysis for ongoing supervision with additional elements beyond those harmonized by the European Banking Authority (EBA), including:

1. Risk-focused supervision: JSTs use a risk-focused approach to identify, assess, and address risks that may pose a threat to financial stability. This approach allows them to prioritize their efforts based on the potential impact of each risk.
2. Supervisory stress tests: JSTs conduct stress tests to evaluate the resilience of supervised entities under adverse economic conditions. These tests help identify vulnerabilities and assess the ability of institutions to absorb shocks.
3. Macroprudential analysis: JSTs analyze the interconnections between financial institutions and the broader economy to identify systemic risks that may threaten financial stability. This analysis helps inform policy decisions aimed at mitigating these risks.
4. Supervisory dialogue: JSTs engage in ongoing dialogue with supervised entities to gather information, assess their risk management practices, and provide guidance on regulatory compliance. This dialogue helps ensure that institutions are aware of the expectations set by the ECB and can take appropriate measures to address any identified issues.
5. On-site inspections: JSTs conduct on-site inspections to verify the accuracy of information provided by supervised entities, assess their risk management practices, and evaluate their compliance with regulatory requirements. These inspections help identify potential weaknesses and areas for improvement in the institutions' operations.
6. Early warning systems: JSTs use early warning systems to monitor developments that may pose a threat to financial stability. These systems help identify trends and patterns that could indicate systemic risks, allowing JSTs to take timely action to address these issues.
7. Collaboration with other supervisory authorities: JSTs collaborate with national competent authorities, as well as international organizations, to share information, coordinate efforts, and leverage expertise in the assessment of risks and the supervision of financial institutions. This collaboration helps ensure a consistent approach to risk assessment across jurisdictions.
8. Use of advanced analytics and modeling techniques: JSTs may use advanced analytics and modeling techniques to enhance their risk assessment capabilities, such as machine learning algorithms, big data analysis, and simulation models. These tools help JSTs identify patterns, trends, and potential risks more efficiently and effectively.

The European Central Bank (ECB) collaborates with the European Banking Authority (EBA) in macroprudential stress tests through the following working structures:

1. Joint Committee of the European Systemic Risk Board (ESRB): The ECB is a member of this committee, which is responsible for macro-prudential oversight at the EU level. The Joint Committee works together to identify, assess, and address systemic risks in the financial sector.
2. Macroprudential Oversight Monitoring Group (MOPG): This group consists of representatives from national macroprudential authorities, the ECB, and the EBA. The MOPG monitors the implementation of macroprudential policies across EU Member States and assesses their effectiveness in addressing systemic risks.
3. European Systemic Risk Board (ESRB): The ESRB is an independent EU body responsible for macro-prudential oversight at the EU level. The ECB, along with other members, contributes to the development of macroprudential policies and oversees their implementation across the EU.
4. Joint Decision-Making Teams (JDMTs): These teams are established by the ECB and national supervisory authorities for specific macroprudential stress tests focusing on system-wide effects. The JDMTs work together to design, conduct, and analyze the results of these stress tests.
5. Macro-prudential Dialogue: The ECB engages in regular dialogue with the EBA and other EU authorities to exchange information, coordinate efforts, and collaborate on macroprudential analysis and policy development. This collaboration helps ensure a consistent approach to macroprudential oversight across the EU.

Additionally, the ECB's macroprudential functions use a wide range of sources, including risk-specific analyses conducted by the horizontal functions of ECB Banking Supervision, the outcomes of the annual ECB stress test and the SREP, as well as institution-specific information through the JSTs. Relevant developments in the economic, regulatory, and supervisory environment and insights from the market intelligence activities of D/SSR also provide important additional input for the assessment. The Supervisory Handbook and guidance developed by the EBA help achieve a high degree of convergence for supervisory practices among participating Member States. In cooperation with the ESRB, the EBA is responsible for the initiation and overall coordination of EU-wide assessments of the resilience of credit institutions as well as the development of common approaches and synergies with EU and euro area-wide banking stress tests. The key features of the microprudential and macroprudential banking supervision framework resulting from the establishment of the SSM are accurately reflected in the documentation emerging from the different FSAP work streams, helping identify areas that need further development in the SSM-wide framework or at national level so that they are appropriately reflected in the IMF recommendations.

Yes, you are correct! The European Central Bank (ECB) follows several steps prior to launching a new data collection that may require additional reporting burden for banks, in accordance with the proportionality principle. These steps include:

1. Assessment of need: The ECB evaluates whether the proposed data collection is necessary and proportionate to achieve its supervisory objectives.
2. Impact assessment: The ECB conducts an impact assessment to determine the potential costs, benefits, and implications for banks in terms of resources, time, and operational efficiency.
3. Consultation with stakeholders: The ECB consults with relevant parties, such as banks, trade associations, and other supervisory authorities, to gather feedback on the proposed data collection and its impact on the banking sector.
4. Proportionality analysis: Based on the results of the impact assessment and consultation, the ECB conducts a proportionality analysis to ensure that the benefits of the data collection outweigh the costs for banks.
5. Adjustment of requirements: If necessary, the ECB may adjust the proposed data collection requirements to minimize the reporting burden on banks while still achieving its supervisory objectives.
6. Communication and implementation: The ECB communicates the finalized data collection requirements to banks and provides guidance on how to comply with them. It also ensures that the implementation of the data collection is consistent across the Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM).
7. Monitoring and review: The ECB monitors the effectiveness of the data collection in achieving its supervisory objectives and reviews it periodically to ensure that it remains proportionate and relevant. It may also adjust the requirements based on feedback from banks and changes in the banking sector or regulatory environment.

Yes, according to the Single Supervisory Mechanism Regulation (SSMR) Articles 9 and 6(5), the European Central Bank (ECB) can employ indirect enforcement measures through National Competent Authorities (NCAs). These measures are implemented under the conditions set out in national law.

Indirect enforcement measures refer to actions taken by NCAs at their discretion, based on ECB decisions or recommendations, to ensure compliance with supervisory requirements. Examples of such measures include:

1. Administrative penalties: NCAs may impose fines or other administrative sanctions on banks that fail to comply with ECB decisions or instructions.
2. Public disclosure: NCAs can publish information about non-compliant banks, warning the public and encouraging market discipline.
3. Withdrawal of authorization: In cases where a bank continues to violate supervisory requirements despite indirect enforcement measures, NCAs may ultimately withdraw its authorization.
4. Restrictions on activities: NCAs can impose restrictions on the activities of non-compliant banks, limiting their ability to engage in certain transactions or business lines.
5. Supervisory guidance and instructions: NCAs can provide additional supervisory guidance and instructions to help banks comply with ECB decisions or requirements, serving as a reminder of their obligations.
6. On-site inspections: NCAs may conduct on-site inspections to verify compliance with supervisory requirements and take appropriate enforcement action if necessary.
7. Reporting requirements: NCAs can require non-compliant banks to submit additional reports or information, helping the ECB monitor their progress towards compliance.

These indirect enforcement measures allow the ECB to exert influence over banks while respecting national legal frameworks and ensuring a consistent approach to supervision across the Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM).

message:
The European Systemic Risk Board (ESRB) determines supervisory priorities based on a comprehensive analysis of various factors that could impact financial stability within the EU. These factors include:

1. Systemic importance: Assessing the systemic importance of financial institutions, markets, and instruments to identify risks that pose the greatest threat to financial stability.
2. Interconnectedness: Evaluating the degree of interconnectedness among financial institutions, markets, and instruments to identify potential contagion risks that could spread across the financial system.
3. Vulnerabilities: Considering the vulnerabilities of the financial system, such as excessive leverage, liquidity mismatches, or concentration risks, which could amplify the impact of shocks on financial stability.
4. Macroeconomic conditions: Taking into account macroeconomic factors, such as GDP growth, inflation, and unemployment rates, to understand the broader economic context in which financial risks are emerging.
5. Regulatory and supervisory frameworks: Assessing the effectiveness of regulatory and supervisory frameworks in addressing identified risks and ensuring financial stability.
6. Policy responses: Evaluating the policy responses taken by national authorities and international organizations to address identified risks and promote financial stability.
7. Data availability and quality: Relying on high-quality data to identify, monitor, and assess risks to financial stability, ensuring that data gaps are addressed and that the data used is reliable and up-to-date.
8. Risk interdependencies: Considering how different risks interact with each other and how they could amplify or mitigate one another's impact on financial stability.
9. Emerging trends and developments: Keeping abreast of emerging trends and developments in the financial system, such as technological advancements, regulatory changes, or market disruptions, to identify potential risks and adjust supervisory priorities accordingly.
10. Stakeholder feedback: Soliciting feedback from relevant stakeholders, including national authorities, international organizations, and industry participants, to ensure that its risk identification and monitoring processes are informed by diverse perspectives and expertise.

By considering these factors, the ESRB can prioritize supervisory actions effectively and promote financial stability in the European Union.

Yes, your understanding of the European Systemic Risk Board (ESRB) is correct. The ESRB is an independent EU body responsible for overseeing risks in the financial system within the EU as a whole. It consists of representatives from national authorities, central banks, and the European Central Bank (ECB), as well as independent members with expertise in various areas relevant to financial stability.

The ESRB works closely with other EU institutions such as the European Banking Authority (EBA) and the European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) to ensure a comprehensive approach to risk identification and management. The ECB supports the ESRB’s Secretariat, and its President is the Chair of the ESRB’s General Board.

The ESRB's role is to identify systemic risks and make recommendations to address them, promoting financial stability across the region. By doing so, it aims to improve the ability of the European Systemic Risk Board to effectively identify, analyze, and monitor EU-wide systemic risks, while the Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM) can take advantage of the ESRB’s expertise, which covers the entire financial system.

In summary, the ESRB is an essential body for ensuring consistent action by the authorities in maintaining financial stability across the European Union. It works closely with other EU institutions to achieve this goal.

message:
The Supervisory Expectations Program (SEP) for a Significant Institution (SI) is a framework used by the European Central Bank (ECB) to assess and monitor the risk management practices of large banks within the Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM). The SEP aims to ensure that SIs maintain robust risk management processes, promote financial stability, and comply with relevant regulatory requirements.

The SEP is structured around three main components:

1. Risk drivers: The ECB identifies key risk drivers for each SI based on factors such as the bank's size, complexity, interconnectedness, and business model. These risk drivers help the ECB focus its supervisory efforts on areas that pose the greatest risks to financial stability.
2. Supervisory priorities: Based on the identified risk drivers, the ECB sets supervisory priorities for each SI. These priorities guide the ECB's engagement with the bank and help ensure that it maintains effective risk management practices.
3. Supervisory dialogue: The ECB engages in ongoing dialogue with SIs to assess their risk management practices, identify areas for improvement, and monitor progress towards addressing any identified issues. This dialogue is based on regular supervisory meetings, on-site inspections, and the exchange of information between the ECB and the bank.

In implementing the SEP, the ECB considers various factors, including:

1. Risk management practices: The ECB assesses the effectiveness of a bank's risk management processes, focusing on areas such as risk identification, assessment, and mitigation.
2. Compliance with regulatory requirements: The ECB evaluates whether the bank is complying with relevant regulatory requirements, including capital adequacy, liquidity, and reporting standards.
3. Internal governance structures: The ECB examines the bank's internal governance structures to ensure that they are effective in managing risks and promoting financial stability.
4. Risk culture: The ECB assesses the bank's risk culture, focusing on factors such as its attitude towards risk-taking, risk awareness, and risk appetite.
5. Operational resilience: The ECB evaluates the bank's operational resilience, looking at areas such as business continuity planning, crisis management, and cybersecurity.
6. Market developments: The ECB takes into account market developments and trends that may impact the bank's risk profile, such as changes in interest rates, credit conditions, or regulatory requirements.
7. Macroeconomic factors: The ECB considers macroeconomic factors, such as GDP growth, inflation, and unemployment rates, to understand the broader economic context in which the bank operates.

By considering these factors, the ECB can effectively implement the SEP for SIs, promoting financial stability within the Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM). The ECB coordinates supervisory cooperation with other EU and national authorities, including negotiations of MoUs, and provides support vis-à-vis bank-specific arrangements. The ECB also participates actively in various global fora, such as the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS) and the Financial Stability Board (FSB). The IMF conducts Systemic Stability Assessments for the euro area, which includes an assessment of the performance of the SSM against the BCBS’s Core Principles for Effective Banking Supervision. The ECB acts as the primary counterpart to the IMF on all issues related to the supervision of SIs.

In the context of banking supervision procedures within the European Union, banks are required to make various formal permission requests or notifications to their national competent authorities (NCAs). These include:

1. Authorization for a new credit institution: A bank seeking to establish a new credit institution within the EU must submit an application to its NCA, which will assess the bank's suitability and grant authorization if it meets the necessary requirements.
2. Notification of significant changes in ownership or control: Banks are required to notify their NCAs of any significant changes in ownership or control, such as acquisitions, mergers, or transfers of shares, that may impact the bank's risk profile or financial stability.
3. Approval for new branches or subsidiaries: A bank wishing to open a new branch or subsidiary within the EU must submit an application to its NCA, which will assess the proposed entity and grant approval if it meets the necessary requirements.
4. Notification of changes in business model or activities: Banks are required to notify their NCAs of any significant changes in their business model or activities that may impact their risk profile or financial stability.
5. Approval for cross-border mergers, acquisitions, or transfers of significant assets: A bank seeking to engage in a cross-border merger, acquisition, or transfer of significant assets must submit an application to its NCA and the relevant authorities in the other EU country involved, which will assess the proposed transaction and grant approval if it meets the necessary requirements.
6. Approval for issuance of covered bonds: A bank wishing to issue covered bonds must obtain approval from its NCA, ensuring that the bonds meet the necessary eligibility criteria and are subject to appropriate supervision.
7. Notification of significant exposures or concentrations: Banks are required to notify their NCAs of any significant exposures or concentrations that may impact their risk profile or financial stability.
8. Approval for large exposures: A bank must obtain approval from its NCA before entering into a large exposure, which is defined as an exposure equal to or greater than 25% of the bank's Tier 1 capital or €30 million, whichever is lower.
9. Notification of material breaches of prudential requirements: Banks are required to notify their NCAs of any material breaches of prudential requirements, such as capital adequacy, liquidity, and reporting standards.
10. Approval for significant increases in the bank's balance sheet or activities: A bank must obtain approval from its NCA before undertaking a significant increase in its balance sheet or activities that may impact its risk profile or financial stability.

To establish a branch or subsidiary within an EU member state, such as France, if you are a bank based outside the European Union (EU), the necessary steps and documentation required would be as follows:

1. Identify the appropriate EU member state where you wish to establish your branch or subsidiary. In this case, it would be France.
2. Prepare a comprehensive business plan outlining the proposed activities, organizational structure, risk management practices, and financial projections of the new entity.
3. Obtain approval from your home country's supervisory authority to establish the branch or subsidiary in the EU. This may involve meeting certain eligibility criteria and providing necessary documentation.
4. Submit an application to the French Prudential Supervision and Resolution Authority (ACPR) for authorization to establish a branch or subsidiary in France. The application should include the business plan, organizational structure, financial statements, and any other relevant information about the bank and its operations.
5. Provide evidence of compliance with the Capital Requirements Regulation (CRR) and the Banking Union's Single Rulebook. This may involve meeting certain capital adequacy, liquidity, and reporting standards.
6. Demonstrate that the proposed entity will not pose a threat to financial stability in France or the EU as a whole. This may involve providing information about the bank's risk management practices, internal controls, and governance structures.
7. If the application is approved, the ACPR will grant authorization for the establishment of the branch or subsidiary in France. The bank will then be subject to ongoing supervision by the ACPR and must comply with all relevant EU regulations and directives.
8. Additionally, it may be helpful to familiarize yourself with any specific guidelines or requirements set forth by the European Central Bank (ECB) for banks operating within the EU, as outlined in their guides on topics such as options and discretions available in EU law, fit and proper assessments, qualifying holding procedures, leveraged transactions, and the treatment of non-performing loans.
9. If the bank is seeking to make use of Decision ECB/2015/4 (the "Decision"), it should also follow the process outlined in the ECB Guide on options and discretions for requesting a waiver from the regulatory conditions for consolidated supervision, as applicable.

These assessments are important in understanding potential risks associated with transactions such as mergers or acquisitions, ensuring that the banks involved meet necessary regulatory requirements. The results can have significant implications for the banks involved, potentially impacting the regulatory approval process, capital requirements, and ongoing supervision of the combined entity. Ad hoc significance assessments help maintain financial stability within the European banking system by identifying and managing risks associated with events outside the normal course of business.

The Supervisory Review and Evaluation Process (SREP) is a framework used by banking supervisors to assess the risk profile of individual banks and determine appropriate prudential requirements. The overall SREP score reflects the supervisor's judgment about the bank's ability to absorb losses and maintain financial stability in adverse economic conditions, taking into account various factors such as credit risk, market risk, operational risk, liquidity risk, and governance risk. This assessment is crucial for setting capital requirements, liquidity standards, and other prudential measures tailored to the bank's specific risk profile, helping ensure that banks maintain adequate levels of capital and liquidity to support their operations and protect depositors and the broader financial system from potential risks. The SREP is executed on a yearly basis but has a multi-year assessment component that is tailored to the risk tolerance defined by each Joint Supervisory Team (JST). In line with the EBA Guidelines on the SREP, the methodology used consists of an assessment carried out by each JST on its own supervised entity based on various factors. The overall SREP score indicates the supervisory view of the overall viability of the institution based on an aggregate view of the threats to viability.

message:
Yes, your understanding of the three main objectives of European banking supervision is correct. The objectives you've listed are:

1. Stability: Supervision aims to maintain financial stability within the banking system by ensuring that individual banks have adequate levels of capital, liquidity, and risk management practices to withstand adverse economic conditions. This helps protect depositors and the broader financial system from potential risks.
2. Solvency: Supervision focuses on assessing a bank's solvency, or its ability to meet its obligations to depositors and other creditors. Regulators evaluate a bank's capital adequacy, liquidity, and asset quality to ensure that it can absorb losses and continue operations in the event of unexpected events.
3. Integrity: Supervision also focuses on maintaining the integrity of the banking system by ensuring that banks comply with relevant laws, regulations, and ethical standards. This includes assessing a bank's governance structure, internal controls, and risk management practices to ensure that it operates transparently and responsibly.

These objectives help create a stable, solvent, and trustworthy banking sector that supports economic growth and financial stability within the European Union.

The ECB may provide information and support during such investigations, but it does not have the power to initiate or conduct criminal investigations itself.

In the context of European banking supervision, regular reporting refers to the periodic submission of financial statements, risk assessments, and other relevant information by banks to their national competent authorities (NCAs) and the European Central Bank (ECB). This includes standardized information on the financial and prudential situation of supervised entities, such as own funds and financial information, liquidity coverage ratio, net stable funding ratio, large exposures, etc. Regular reporting is based on the EBA reporting framework or an ECB regulation/decision.

Ad hoc reporting, on the other hand, refers to the submission of additional or one-off reports in response to specific events or changes that may impact a bank's risk profile. These events can include mergers, acquisitions, significant increases or decreases in risk exposure, or other unusual circumstances outside the normal course of business. Ad hoc reporting provides supervisors with a more detailed understanding of these events and their potential impact on the bank's financial stability.

Both regular and ad hoc reporting are essential components of European banking supervision, as they help ensure that banks maintain adequate levels of capital, liquidity, and risk management practices to support their operations and protect depositors and the broader financial system from potential risks.

In the European Union, the Single Resolution Mechanism (SRM) and the Bank Recovery and Resolution Directive (BRRD) are the primary frameworks for resolving failing banks without burdening taxpayers. The Single Resolution Board (SRB) is responsible for implementing resolution schemes for significant credit institutions in the Eurozone, while national resolution authorities handle non-significant credit institutions and other financial institutions within their respective Member States. The European Central Bank (ECB) provides technical assistance to these authorities.

In case of a failing bank, the resolution authority will take measures to restore its viability or wind it down in an orderly manner, minimizing negative impacts on financial stability and protecting depositors' funds. This may involve asset sales, transfer of operations to a bridge institution, or bail-in of debt instruments to recapitalize the bank. The SRM centralizes key competences and resources for financial assistance to directly recapitalize financial institutions under certain conditions. Effective cooperation and robust information flows between the SSM, ESM, and national resolution authorities are crucial for the successful implementation of these resolution schemes.

message:
To ensure fairness, transparency, and integrity when opening a new branch in France under European banking supervision, your bank should adhere to the following key principles regarding conflicts of interest:

1. Identification: Recognize potential conflicts of interest that may arise between the bank's various activities, clients, or stakeholders by establishing clear guidelines for employees and monitoring transactions and relationships.
2. Disclosure: Ensure transparency by disclosing any actual or potential conflicts of interest to all relevant parties, including clients, regulators, and other stakeholders.
3. Mitigation: Implement measures to minimize the impact of conflicts of interest on the bank's decision-making processes and operations, such as separating conflicting activities, establishing firewalls between different business units, or imposing restrictions on transactions.
4. Management: Establish procedures for managing conflicts of interest that cannot be eliminated, including setting up committees to review and approve transactions with potential conflicts and ensuring decisions are made in the best interests of all parties involved.
5. Prevention: Implement policies and controls to prevent future conflicts of interest from arising through regular training for employees on conflict-of-interest issues, establishing clear guidelines for business conduct, and monitoring transactions and relationships to identify potential conflicts early.

It is important to note that the Autorité de Contrôle Prudentiel et de Résolution (ACPR), which serves as the French National Competent Authority (NCA) for banking supervision, expects banks to have robust governance policies in place to manage conflicts of interest effectively. Compliance with these policies is crucial for maintaining trust and ensuring financial stability within the European banking system.

Yes, a bank's status as either a Less Significant Institution (LSI) or a Significant Institution (SI) can change based on the evolution of its size, interconnectedness, complexity, and cross-border activities. The European Central Bank (ECB) and national competent authorities (NCAs) monitor these factors to determine whether a bank should be reclassified as an LSI or SI.

A bank may be reclassified as an SI if it experiences significant growth in terms of assets, deposits, or other relevant indicators, becomes more interconnected with other financial institutions, introduces new products, services, or business lines that increase its complexity, or expands its operations into other European countries to a certain extent. Conversely, a bank may be reclassified as an LSI if it experiences a significant decline in size, interconnectedness, complexity, or cross-border activities.

The ECB and NCAs will monitor these factors on an ongoing basis to ensure that banks are appropriately classified based on their risk profile and the need for effective supervision. If the failure of an LSI has the potential to significantly impact the stability of the domestic or area-wide financial system, or if the institution is intrinsically risky, the ECB may take over its direct supervision. The ECB assesses several indicators for each institution, such as size, importance for the local economy, cross-border activities, and business model to determine the potential impact of an LSI on the system. LSIs with the highest potential impact are classified as "high-impact LSIs".

message: The benchmarking process in banking supervision involves comparing a bank's performance against its peers within the same market or industry, using common metrics such as capital adequacy ratios, liquidity ratios, and risk-weighted assets. This comparison helps supervisors identify strengths, weaknesses, and potential risks that may not be apparent when evaluating individual banks in isolation. The tolerance framework, on the other hand, sets limits on various prudential indicators for banks to ensure they maintain adequate levels of financial strength and resilience. If a bank's performance falls below these thresholds, it may be required to take corrective actions to address the identified concerns and bring its performance back within the tolerance framework. This helps supervisors maintain financial stability within the banking system by ensuring that banks are well-capitalized and able to withstand shocks or adverse events.

In a college of supervisors where the European Central Bank (ECB) serves as both the host supervisor and the consolidating supervisor, the roles are as follows:

1. Host Supervisor (ECB): The ECB is responsible for coordinating the work of the college, ensuring that all members have access to the necessary information, and facilitating discussions on the supervised entity's activities, risks, and compliance with prudential requirements. The host supervisor also ensures that decisions made by the college are consistent with the Single Supervisory Mechanism (SSM) Regulation and other relevant EU legislation.

2. Consolidating Supervisor (ECB): As the consolidating supervisor, the ECB is responsible for overseeing the consolidated supervision of a banking group, which involves assessing the group's overall risk profile, ensuring that all subsidiaries comply with prudential requirements, and coordinating the work of the other national supervisors involved in the group's supervision. The ECB also ensures that the consolidated supervision is consistent with the SSM Regulation and other relevant EU legislation.

In this scenario, the Joint Supervisory Team (JST) coordinator plays a crucial role in facilitating communication between the ECB and the national competent authorities involved in the college of supervisors. The JST coordinator ensures that all members have access to the necessary information, coordinates discussions on the supervised entity's activities, risks, and compliance with prudential requirements, and helps to ensure that decisions made by the college are consistent with the SSM Regulation and other relevant EU legislation.

message: The European Central Bank (ECB) plays a crucial role in ensuring the adequacy and effectiveness of the Single Resolution Fund (SRF). Although it does not directly manage the SRF, the ECB collaborates closely with the Single Resolution Board (SRB), which is responsible for managing the fund. The ECB consults the SRB on proposed ex-ante contributions to be paid by significant institutions into the SRF and provides an advisory role in cases of resolution. Additionally, the ECB's Business Model, Capital & Crisis Division contributes to cooperation arrangements with other European authorities to ensure effective planning, decision-making, and coordination at both the European and national levels. The target level of the SRF is set at 1% of the total deposits held by credit institutions in participating Member States, aiming to provide a source of funding for resolving failing banks without requiring additional contributions from national budgets or taxpayers.

Risk identification and monitoring in banking supervision plays a crucial role in maintaining financial stability by helping supervisors anticipate potential risks and challenges that may arise in the future. By adopting a forward-looking approach, supervisors can identify emerging trends, assess their impact on the financial stability of banks, and take proactive measures to mitigate these risks before they become material. This proactive stance helps reduce the likelihood of crises or disruptions within the banking system.

To achieve this, risk identification and monitoring in banking supervision takes a forward-looking perspective by considering factors such as macroeconomic trends, changes in market conditions, technological advancements, and shifts in business models. Supervisors use various tools and methodologies to assess these factors, including stress testing, scenario analysis, thematic reviews, and on-site inspections. By incorporating a forward-looking perspective into their risk identification and monitoring processes, supervisors can ensure that banks are adequately prepared for future challenges and maintain the stability of the financial system as a whole.

In banking supervision, the subsets of data being referred to typically consist of granular information related to individual banks or groups of banks, which may not be included in publicly available aggregate data. These subsets can include detailed financial statements, risk assessments, and internal control evaluations that are collected during on-site inspections or through regulatory reporting.

These subsets of data are often used by supervisors to gain a more comprehensive understanding of the financial health, risk profile, and compliance status of individual banks. They provide insights into specific areas such as capital adequacy, liquidity management, asset quality, and governance practices that may not be apparent from aggregate data alone.

These subsets of data are closely related to other published data in that they form the basis for the analysis and conclusions drawn by supervisors when assessing the overall stability of the banking system. The insights gained from these subsets help inform decisions on policy-making, risk management, and supervisory actions, ultimately contributing to the maintenance of financial stability at both the individual bank level and across the broader banking sector.

Pending proceedings can also impact a member's ability to commit sufficient time to their functions, which should be taken into account during the evaluation process. The supervised entity should have governance policies in place for identifying, disclosing, mitigating, managing, and preventing conflicts of interest. By adhering to these principles, management bodies can function effectively and make decisions in the best interests of the institutions they oversee.
